key_finding: deltaHED, a metric quantifying evolutionary divergence between germline and tumor-acquired HLA class I alleles, was associated with higher TMB and neoantigen load yet predicted worse PFS and OS under PD-1 blockade across three cancer types (RM/NPC, melanoma, ESCC). In ESCC the association was specific to the immunotherapy arm, and high deltaHED correlated with more mutations in antigen-processing and TCR pathways.
intervention: PD-1 blockade (monotherapy in RM/NPC and melanoma; PD-1 + chemotherapy in ESCC)
outcome: High deltaHED predicted worse PFS and OS on PD-1 blockade despite higher TMB/neoantigen load
effect_size: 3 cohorts: n=164 (RM/NPC POLARIS-02), n=88 (melanoma), n=477 (ESCC JUPITER-06); survival difference specific to the immunotherapy arm of ESCC

confidence.reasoning: Three independent cohorts including two randomized trials, but findings are retrospective biomarker analyses without functional validation; effect direction is contrary to prior HED literature and needs prospective confirmation.

caveats: Retrospective biomarker analysis
deltaHED combines germline and somatic HLA divergence; attribution between components is not fully resolved
Mechanistic link to antigen-processing mutations is correlative
ESCC cohort received combination with chemotherapy, complicating interpretation
surprising_why: Contradicts the prevailing TMB/neoantigen-centric view: although high deltaHED tracked with higher TMB and neoantigen burden, it predicted worse rather than better PD-1 outcomes, suggesting HLA evolutionary divergence can mark immune escape rather than broad presentation capacity.

key_finding: Single-cell spatial transcriptomics of 27 cSCC patients across three cohorts identified six distinct tissue niches that stratify response to neoadjuvant PD-1/PD-L1 blockade: high antigen presentation, B/plasma cell, and inflammatory keratinocyte niches were enriched in responders, while proliferative keratinocyte, low-antigen-presentation myeloid, and fibroblast-rich EMT niches dominated non-responders. Pretreatment niche profiling outperformed PD-L1 status in predicting pathologic response.
intervention: Neoadjuvant PD-1/PD-L1 blockade in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma
outcome: Six spatial niches differentially enriched in responders vs non-responders; niche profiling outperformed PD-L1 IHC
effect_size: 27 patients across three cohorts (including two phase II trials); qualitative advantage of niche-based prediction over PD-L1 status

confidence.reasoning: Includes two phase II trials and multi-timepoint sampling, but total sample size is modest (27 patients) and validation is internal across the three cohorts; findings are associative.

caveats: Small cohort (n=27)
Tissue microarray cores (1.7 mm) may not capture full tumor heterogeneity
Observational associations; no causal perturbation of niches
cSCC-specific — generalization untested

key_finding: Transient upregulation of PD-1 on clonally expanding human CD4+ and CD8+ T cells protects them from premature restimulation-induced cell death (RICD) by attenuating proximal TCR and CD28 signaling and modulating pro/anti-apoptotic protein expression. PD-L1 engagement rescued effector T cells from RICD in a dose- and synapse-proximity-dependent manner, with stronger rescue under CD28 co-ligation.
intervention: PD-L1-coated bead restimulation of expanding human primary T cells; biochemical and proteomic profiling of TCR/CD28 signaling
outcome: PD-1 signaling conferred apoptosis resistance to restimulated effector T cells, particularly PD-1^hi clonally expanding populations
effect_size: Qualitative; dose-dependent PD-L1 rescue of RICD in human primary T cells; no clinical or in vivo quantification

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistic human primary T cell study with biochemical/proteomic support, but confined to in vitro systems without in vivo or patient validation.

caveats: In vitro human T cell system only
No in vivo tumor or patient data
Mechanistic implications for PD-1 blockade-induced T cell loss are inferred
surprising_why: Inverts the traditional framing of PD-1 as a purely death/exhaustion-inducing receptor: homeostatic PD-1 expression protects newly expanding effector T cells from apoptosis, implying anti-PD-1 therapy could paradoxically sensitize certain T cell populations to RICD and alter adaptive immune durability in ways not captured by the simple checkpoint-release model.

key_finding: T cell-intrinsic VISTA enforces CD8+ T cell dysfunction: its deletion enhanced early CD8+ priming and expansion but did not yield durable tumor control because trans-VISTA on myeloid cells and compensatory CTLA-4 upregulation sustained dysfunction. Combining T cell-intrinsic VISTA loss with CTLA-4 blockade improved T cell survival, broadened TCR diversity, and produced stronger tumor regression than CTLA-4 blockade alone; a VISTA-deficient cytotoxic T cell signature correlated with favorable ICI outcomes in patients.
intervention: T cell-specific VISTA deletion in tumor-bearing mice, with and without CTLA-4 blockade
outcome: Enhanced early CD8+ priming/expansion with VISTA loss; synergy with anti-CTLA-4 producing robust tumor regression and broader TCR repertoire
effect_size: Qualitative improvement over anti-CTLA-4 monotherapy; patient correlation with existing ICI outcomes reported

confidence.reasoning: Integrates scRNA-seq, TCR repertoire, and flow cytometry in mice plus a patient correlation, but durability advantage requires combination with CTLA-4 blockade.

caveats: Preclinical mouse models; no clinical trial
Patient correlation is retrospective signature analysis
Direct relevance to anti-PD-1 resistance inferred rather than directly tested

key_finding: In dMMR/MSI-H metastatic colorectal cancer, an 8-gene (4 k-TSP pair) classifier combined with mucinous histology identifies a subgroup (Cluster A + mucinous; 15.3% of patients) that derives substantially greater benefit from anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4 combination versus anti-PD-1-based monotherapy. This composite biomarker improved model fit for a treatment interaction across 163 pooled patients from two independent cohorts.
intervention: Anti-PD-1 ± anti-LAG-3 (Mono) vs anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4 (Combo) in dMMR/MSI-H mCRC
outcome: Cluster A + mucinous subgroup showed a large PFS benefit from Combo over Mono
effect_size: Pooled n=163; k-TSP balanced accuracy 68.1%; adjusted 24-month PFS 72.2% (Combo) vs 13.8% (Mono) in biomarker-positive subgroup; HR 0.10 (95% CI 0.02–0.39; p<0.001)

confidence.reasoning: Training plus independent validation cohort with pre-specified composite biomarker and sizeable HR, but the benefiting subgroup is small (25 patients) and analyses are retrospective across non-randomized comparisons.

caveats: Retrospective analysis; not a randomized comparison
Biomarker-positive subgroup is small (n=25)
Excluded patients on anti-PD-L1 and ECOG ≥2
Prospective validation pending

key_finding: HILPDA sustains KLF5-driven fatty acid synthesis, generating palmitate that augments PD-L1 palmitoylation at cysteine 272 to stabilize membrane PD-L1 and drive immune evasion; TRIM21 catalyzes K63-linked polyubiquitination and degradation of HILPDA, and pharmacologic TRIM21 engagement with fenretinide restores anti-PD-1 efficacy in breast cancer models.
intervention: Fenretinide (TRIM21 engager) to degrade HILPDA, combined with anti-PD-1
outcome: Reduced PD-L1 palmitoylation, increased cytotoxic immunity, improved anti-PD-1 efficacy in breast cancer models
effect_size: Qualitative: restored antitumor responses in mouse breast cancer models

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistically detailed mouse and cell-line study with defined molecular axis (HILPDA-HSP90-KLF5-palmitate-PD-L1 C272); lacks clinical validation and relies on a single tumor type.

caveats: Breast cancer models only
Fenretinide specificity for TRIM21 engagement not fully characterized
No clinical validation
surprising_why: Identifies a novel post-translational modification axis in which lipid-droplet regulator HILPDA drives PD-L1 palmitoylation via KLF5-sustained fatty acid synthesis, linking tumor lipogenesis directly to ICB resistance through a PTM rather than transcriptional PD-L1 regulation.

key_finding: NOTCH3 transcriptionally upregulates the immune checkpoint PVR via RBPJ binding in colorectal cancer, suppressing CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity through enhanced PVR-TIGIT interactions; NOTCH3 depletion synergizes with anti-PD-L1 in mice, and NOTCH3 mutation or low expression independently predicts improved survival in ICB-treated CRC and pan-cancer cohorts.
outcome: Inhibited tumor growth and increased CD8+ T cell infiltration in mouse xenografts; improved survival in ICB-treated patients with NOTCH3 mutation/low expression
effect_size: Qualitative; 102-patient institutional cohort plus MSKCC validation

confidence.reasoning: Multi-modal study with TCGA multi-omics, scRNA-seq, in vitro mechanistic assays, mouse model, and two clinical cohorts; anti-NOTCH3 intervention is genetic, not yet pharmacologic.

caveats: No pharmacologic NOTCH3 inhibitor tested in vivo
Clinical correlations are retrospective

key_finding: PKMYT1 is overexpressed in castration-resistant prostate cancer and suppresses antitumor immunity; its inhibition with RP-6306 activates the cGAS-STING pathway, potentiates type I/II IFN signaling, upregulates CCL5 and CXCL10, and enhances CD8+ T cell infiltration and anti-PD-L1 efficacy in syngeneic mouse models.
intervention: PKMYT1 inhibitor RP-6306 alone or combined with anti-PD-L1
outcome: Activated cGAS-STING, increased activated CD8+ T cell infiltration, significant tumor suppression in vivo
effect_size: Qualitative: significant tumor suppression in syngeneic mouse CRPC models

confidence.reasoning: Combines bioinformatic analyses, IHC in a clinical PCa cohort, mechanistic cell/mouse studies, and scRNA-seq; no prospective clinical data.

caveats: Preclinical efficacy only
cGAS-STING activation mechanism downstream of PKMYT1 inhibition not fully dissected
Generalization beyond CRPC not addressed

key_finding: Lactate drives AARS1-catalyzed lysine lactylation of PD-L1 at K280 in its intracellular domain, which blocks HUWE1-mediated ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, thereby stabilizing PD-L1 and impairing CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity; sodium lactate enhances anti-PD-L1 efficacy in preclinical models and K280 lactylation correlates with advanced NSCLC stage and poor survival.
intervention: Sodium lactate (NaLa) administration combined with anti-PD-L1; genetic perturbation of AARS1
outcome: Enhanced efficacy of anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy; clinical correlation with advanced NSCLC stage and poor survival
effect_size: Qualitative; clinical association with NSCLC stage and survival

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistically detailed with identification of lactyltransferase (AARS1), specific residue (K280), and competing E3 ligase (HUWE1); supported by in vivo mouse data and NSCLC clinical correlations.

caveats: NSCLC clinical cohort size not specified
Paradoxical finding that exogenous lactate enhances anti-PD-L1 efficacy requires careful mechanistic reconciliation
No clinical trial of lactate plus ICB
surprising_why: Reveals that PD-L1 is stabilized by lysine lactylation at K280 which blocks HUWE1-mediated ubiquitination, a previously unappreciated metabolite-driven PTM linking tumor microenvironment lactate to ICB resistance, and counterintuitively implies that exogenous lactate can enhance anti-PD-L1 efficacy.

key_finding: IFN-γ activates IRF1, which transcriptionally upregulates AGPAT3, driving lipid remodeling and accumulation of polyunsaturated ether phospholipids that sensitize tumor cells to ferroptosis; loss of AGPAT3 impairs IFN-γ-mediated tumor elimination, and higher tumor AGPAT3 expression is associated with improved survival in ICI-treated patients.
outcome: AGPAT3 loss impaired IFN-γ-mediated tumor killing in vitro and in vivo; higher AGPAT3 associated with immune activation and improved OS under ICI
effect_size: Qualitative: improved OS in ICI-treated patients with higher AGPAT3

confidence.reasoning: Integrates machine learning on ferroptosis gene signatures, scRNA-seq, lipidomics, ChIP-seq/CUT&Tag, and in vitro/in vivo validation.

caveats: ICI-treated cohort details not specified
Causal role of ether phospholipids in ferroptosis sensitization inferred from lipidomics
No pharmacologic AGPAT3 modulator tested

key_finding: In NSCLC, hMENA overexpression defines a TGF-β-driven myofibroblast-like CAF subset in which hMENA and TGF-β signaling reciprocally reinforce each other, driving PD-L1 upregulation, EMT, ECM remodeling, and secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines that expand Tregs and impair CD8+/CD4+ T-cell function. A 9-gene signature derived from this CAF subtype correlates with poor prognosis in TCGA NSCLC and with ICT resistance in SU2C and the OAK phase III trial.
intervention: hMENA silencing in CAFs; derivation of a 9-gene ICT-response signature
outcome: hMENA-high CAFs sustain TGF-β signaling and EMT, upregulate PD-L1, expand Tregs, and associate with poor prognosis and anti-PD-L1/anti-PD-1 resistance
effect_size: Qualitative; signature correlates with ICT resistance in SU2C and OAK (NCT02008227) cohorts

confidence.reasoning: Integrates single-cell atlas, ex vivo CAF experiments, and validation in multiple human cohorts including a phase III trial, but mechanistic work is largely correlative/observational.

caveats: Signature validation is retrospective
Mechanistic causality in patients inferred from in vitro CAF experiments
Abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes

key_finding: In triple-negative breast cancer, TROP2 associates with claudin 7 to regulate tight junctions and create a barrier that excludes T cells; TROP2 loss deregulates tight junction proteins and enables CD8+ T-cell infiltration, while TROP2-directed therapy (hRS7) enhances anti-PD-1 efficacy in a humanized TROP2 syngeneic model and TROP2 expression correlates with lack of response to anti-PD-1 in human breast cancer.
intervention: TROP2 loss-of-function and reconstitution; hRS7 (sacituzumab govitecan antibody component) combined with anti-PD-1
outcome: TROP2 loss or targeting improves T-cell infiltration and effector function and enhances anti-PD-1 response; high TROP2 predicts anti-PD-1 non-response
effect_size: Qualitative: TROP2 inversely correlated with T-cell infiltration

confidence.reasoning: Combines spatial transcriptomics, functional perturbation, syngeneic humanized mouse model, and human ICB data.

caveats: Human ICB correlation cohort size not specified
Preclinical combination efficacy in a single humanized TROP2 syngeneic TNBC model
surprising_why: Reframes TROP2, known primarily as an ADC target, as a functional driver of barrier-mediated immune exclusion via tight-junction regulation, providing a mechanistic rationale beyond cytotoxic payload delivery for combining TROP2 ADCs with anti-PD-1.

key_finding: In microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer, M2-like tumor-associated macrophages express elevated COX1/2 and produce PGE2 that drives TIGIT upregulation on PD-1+ CD8+ T cells, generating a terminally exhausted phenotype that blunts anti-PD-L1 efficacy. M2-TAM depletion, COX2/PGE2-receptor inhibition, or TIGIT blockade reduces TIGIT, restores CD8+ T-cell function, and improves PD-L1 blockade activity; human CRC transcriptomic data corroborate the TAM-COX-TIGIT axis.
intervention: Anti-PD-L1; M2-like TAM depletion; COX2 inhibition; PGE2 receptor inhibition; TIGIT blockade (combinations)
outcome: Reduced TIGIT expression on CD8+ T cells, restored cytotoxicity, improved anti-PD-L1 efficacy in MSS models
effect_size: Qualitative improvement in CD8+ function and anti-PD-L1 efficacy

confidence.reasoning: Mechanism established in mouse models with multiple orthogonal perturbations and supported by single-cell, spatial, and bulk human CRC transcriptomics, though clinical validation is correlative.

caveats: Preclinical models; no clinical trial data
Human data are correlative transcriptomic associations

key_finding: Low-protein diet-induced hypoalbuminemia in C57BL/6 LLC tumor-bearing mice drives resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors by impairing macrophage arginine biosynthesis, producing an immunosuppressive TME with reduced CD8+ T-cell infiltration and expanded TAMs. TAM depletion or dietary arginine supplementation reverses the immunosuppressive phenotype and restores ICI efficacy.
intervention: Low-protein diet; anti-PD-1/PD-L1 ICI; TAM depletion; dietary arginine supplementation
outcome: Hypoalbuminemia reduced ICI response; TAM depletion or arginine supplementation restored CD8+ infiltration and ICI efficacy

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistic mouse study with multiple complementary perturbations, but limited to a single syngeneic LLC model without clinical validation.

caveats: Single mouse tumor model (LLC)
Dietary manipulation may not fully recapitulate cancer cachexia biology
No patient-level intervention data
surprising_why: Establishes hypoalbuminemia not merely as a prognostic biomarker but as a direct causal driver of ICI resistance via a macrophage arginine-metabolism circuit, suggesting a nutritional/metabolic intervention (arginine repletion) could restore immunotherapy response.

key_finding: In the phase 3 ATOMIC trial (NCT02912559), adding atezolizumab to adjuvant mFOLFOX6 chemotherapy significantly improved 3-year disease-free survival in resected stage III mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) colon cancer compared with mFOLFOX6 alone.
intervention: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) plus mFOLFOX6 for 6 months, then atezolizumab monotherapy to 12 months total
outcome: Improved 3-year disease-free survival versus chemotherapy alone, at the cost of more grade 3/4 adverse events

confidence.reasoning: Randomized phase 3 trial, 712 patients, prespecified primary endpoint, 40.9-month median follow-up, strong effect size with tight CI.

caveats: Overall survival data not yet mature
Increased grade 3/4 toxicity
Only stage III dMMR subset; does not generalize to MMR-proficient disease

key_finding: In ICI-refractory stage IV melanoma patients, intratumoral LOAd703 (a TMZ-CD40L/4-1BBL-expressing oncolytic adenovirus) plus atezolizumab reprogrammed the myeloid compartment and T-cell landscape, producing local and systemic immune signatures previously associated with ICI response.
intervention: Intratumoral LOAd703 (1e11 or 5e11 viral particles) plus intravenous atezolizumab (1200 mg) every 3 weeks
outcome: Increased TME DC-associated biomarkers, more circulating CD40+ DC-like cells, elevated T-cell infiltration signatures, rise in EM-phenotype CD8+ T cells, and reduced circulating CD4+ T cells including Tregs
effect_size: Qualitative biomarker shifts; n=24 patients in single-arm phase I/II

confidence.reasoning: Single-arm phase I/II, small sample, biomarker-focused endpoints rather than clinical response.

caveats: Single-arm design without randomized comparator
Primary readouts are correlative immune biomarkers, not objective response or survival
Heterogeneous prior-therapy exposure
surprising_why: Demonstrates that myeloid reprogramming via a CD40L/4-1BBL-armed oncolytic vector can restore an ICI-responsive immune signature in melanoma patients with confirmed anti-PD-1 resistance — a population where few interventions reliably re-sensitize to checkpoint blockade.

key_finding: In a phase Ib trial (NCT05400876), dual checkpoint blockade with anti-TIM-3 antibody TQB2618 plus anti-PD-1 penpulimab produced clinically meaningful responses in relapsed/refractory classical Hodgkin lymphoma patients who had already failed prior PD-1/PD-L1 therapy.
intervention: TQB2618 (anti-TIM-3, 600 mg Q3W RP2D) plus penpulimab (anti-PD-1, 200 mg Q3W)
outcome: Objective responses in a post-PD-1-failure cHL population with manageable toxicity
effect_size: ORR 52% (1 CR, 10 PRs) among 21 r/r cHL patients; TRAEs in 86%, grade ≥3 TRAEs in 24%; median DOR and OS not reached at 14.1-month median follow-up

confidence.reasoning: Multicenter phase Ib, small sample (n=21 efficacy-evaluable cHL), single-arm, early follow-up.

caveats: Small single-arm phase Ib cohort
No randomized comparator
DOR and OS not yet reached
Conducted at Chinese sites only
surprising_why: Achieves a 52% ORR by adding TIM-3 blockade to anti-PD-1 in cHL patients who had already progressed on prior PD-1/PD-L1 therapy — a setting where re-engaging checkpoint biology was not expected to yield substantial salvage responses.

key_finding: KLRG1 is upregulated on CD8+ T cells after checkpoint therapy and high KLRG1 expression correlates with anti-PD-1 resistance in melanoma; KLRG1 or N-cadherin (ligand) loss slows tumor growth through CD8+ T, NK, and gamma-delta T cells, and a novel anti-human KLRG1 mAb reduces tumor progression in humanized KLRG1 knock-in mice.
intervention: KLRG1 genetic knockout, tumor N-cadherin deletion, and a novel anti-human KLRG1 monoclonal antibody in murine and humanized-mouse models
outcome: Reduced tumor growth and enhanced human T-cell activation; anti-PD-1-resistant melanoma patient tumors show high KLRG1 on TILs

confidence.reasoning: Preclinical mouse study with converging genetic, ligand, and antibody evidence plus patient correlative data; no clinical efficacy yet.

caveats: Predominantly murine and humanized-mouse data
Patient component is correlative, not interventional
No in-human efficacy or safety data
surprising_why: Identifies KLRG1 as a tractable, previously underappreciated inhibitory receptor acting across CD8+ T, NK, and gamma-delta T compartments whose blockade shows therapeutic activity in anti-PD-1-refractory settings, nominating a novel checkpoint axis (KLRG1-cadherin) beyond the established PD-1/CTLA-4/LAG-3/TIM-3 targets.
